posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Rhea Subbaiah’s (@rhea.subbaiah) wedding to Nikhil Bidapa (@nikhilbidapa) was a beautiful celebration of family, tradition and the people who shaped their story.
As Rhea puts it, “Looking back, it feels like we were always meant to find our way to each other.”
The couple had technically known each other for most of their lives, but it was Bengaluru that played cupid. Their two-day Kodava wedding brought together generations of family, meaningful rituals, heirloom jewellery, traditional music, endless dancing and the kind of warmth only a close-knit community can create.
Dressed in timeless Kanchipuram silks and heirloom Kodava jewellery passed down through generations, Rhea’s bridal looks were rooted in heritage. But what truly stood out was how personal the celebrations felt. No priests, no elaborate formalities—just the blessings of their ancestors, elders and loved ones.
Read more via the link in bio 🔗
Words: Ismat Tahseen (@ismat.tahseen)
#Manifest #Wedding
[Wedding Diary, Real Wedding]

posts.caption:
Some TV weddings are so iconic, they become part of our collective love story.
These are the wedding episodes we still revisit years later, because the best TV weddings were never just about the wedding décor, bridal fashion or dreamy venues. They were about the love stories we invested seasons in, the couples we rooted for, and the happy endings that had us laughing, crying and falling in love right alongside them.
From the slow-burn romance of Anthony Bridgerton and Kate Sharma to the chaos and charm of Monica and Chandler’s big day, the heartfelt magic of Jim and Pam’s Niagara Falls wedding, and Amy Santiago and Jake Peralta’s unforgettable Brooklyn Nine-Nine ceremony, these are the wedding episodes that set the standard for romance on screen.
Whether you’re building your wedding mood board, searching for inspiration, or simply rewatching your favourite comfort show, these are the TV weddings we keep coming back to.

posts.caption:
Is the bridal gold anklet making a comeback?
As modern brides embrace a more personal approach to wedding dressing, the gold anklet is quietly re-emerging as an elegant accessory that sits at the intersection of tradition, symbolism and style.
For generations, the bridal jewellery checklist has remained largely unchanged: necklaces, earrings, bangles, a maang tikka and perhaps a nath. Yet one accessory that once held a place of pride in a bride’s trousseau has gradually slipped out of the spotlight—the anklet.
Now, gold anklets are making a comeback in bridal fashion. From cultural beliefs and astrological perspectives to celebrity influence and contemporary styling, their revival raises an interesting question: should brides be wearing gold anklets on their wedding day?
The answer, unsurprisingly, depends on whom you ask.
Read more via the link in bio🔗
By Kratagya Rathore (@kratagya_rathore)
#Manifest
[Bridal Fashion 2026, Modern Bride, Wedding Trends, Gold Jewellery, Wedding Inspiration]

posts.caption:
Last year, the designer Mike Amiri encountered the soccer effect for the first time.
F.C. Barcelona, the Spanish soccer team that has won the Champions League five times and is contributing 16 players to various World Cup teams, announced that Amiri would be its official formal wear partner for the next five years. The result of the announcement, Amiri said, trumped the effect of any of the celebrities — like Justin Bieber, Teyana Taylor and Maluma — who wear his label.
“We were a niche Los Angeles brand, doing shows in Paris, and then all of a sudden we became global,” Amiri said. Athletes like Travis Kelce, Kyle Kuzma and Odell Beckham Jr. have gravitated toward the label, not only because of the fit of the clothes, but also because Amiri took an interest in sports stars when the fashion industry ignored them.
Now, as the World Cup begins and fashion wises up to the ways that soccer can elevate brands, Amiri may be a case study in what happens next. Tap the link in our bio to read more about how @amiri is leveling up. Photos by @theirnameisricardo, Amiri, Chris Graythen/Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 13, 2026. May be an image of crowd and text.

There was also a red, white and blue Liberty flame hat, some sombreros, and at least one pair of fans dressed in Stars and Stripes overalls.

posts.caption:
David Hockney, the artist who died on Thursday at 88, had a madcap maximalism in his approach to style. As with his paintings, he favored bold colors for his clothes.
"It always seemed as if there were another Hockney outfit to discover," the fashion reporter Jacob Gallagher wrote. His style pinged around the mood boards of the fashion world for decades, showing the painter at every life stage: the young Londoner in his pink-striped rugby shirt and pulverized plimsolls, or the still whimsical Pop Art icon in mod tweed suits well into his 80s. Hockney became a north star for designers trying to get men to wear more color.
"When it comes to style, some people just have it," @jacobwgallagher wrote. "It seems to emanate from their very being. Hockney was that kind of man." Read more about Hockney's style legacy and see more photos at the link in our bio. Photos by @nathanael.turner, Anthony Barboza/Getty Images, Aaron Chown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images, @suzanne_plunkett
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 14, 2026. May be an image of suit, magazine, outerwear, sportswear and text that says 'ခင် How Amiri Built a a Fashion Brand on the Culture of Sports'.

posts.caption:
Knicks fans don’t settle for simple T-shirts. (Not even, to the team’s dismay, free ones.)
They want to show their pride, yes, of course. But they’re not willing to squelch their sense of style entirely. This is New York after all.
On Monday night, as the Knicks had their first home finals game since Rudy Giuliani was mayor, fans have been coated in orange and blue.  Beyond that committed color scheme, though, just about anything goes.
Swipe through to see the style at Madison Square Garden and tap the link in our bio to see more photos. Photos by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
On Monday evening, New Yorkers huddled across the boroughs to watch the Knicks play the Spurs in Game 3 of the NBA finals. Some celebrities, including Spike Lee and Timothée Chalamet, snagged courtside seats, but downtown, others joined an unexpected group of Knicks fans for the 19th annual Chanel Tribeca Festival Artists Dinner.
Stars like Whoopi Goldberg, Sofia Coppola and Robert de Niro gathered in in the former Tribeca Grill space for the event that honored 10 artists who donated works that are presented to winners of the Tribeca Festival, which kicked off last week.
Just before tip-off, five big-screen TVs stationed around the room were powered up. There were napkins with the Chanel logo embroidered in orange and blue and trays of black-and-white cookies — made instead with orange-and-blue frosting — floated around the room alongside Knicks caps stacked on silver trays.
“We’ve been waiting for this moment,” the actress Teyana Taylor told The New York Times reporter @sophiajune. “I feel like a kid again.” At the link in our bio see more photos from inside the party. Photos by @kristaschlueter
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 14, 2026. May be an image of suit, magazine, outerwear, sportswear and text that says 'ခင် How Amiri Built a a Fashion Brand on the Culture of Sports'.

posts.caption:
First debuted for Fall 2024, the Robe Spirale was a feat of fashion engineering. At its core was a 3D-printed structure formed as one continuous coiled strip. Like a slap bracelet, it wrapped and snapped around the torso, using the natural curves of the body to hold itself securely in place.
#PieterMulier further developed the idea for Spring 2025 with a series of pleated dresses that appeared to float on the body. They gripped the torso at carefully calculated points, while hidden internal armatures carried much of the structural load. The visible fabric acted as the skin of the construction, and the lightweight pleating reduced the need for heavy internal support.
Just like #AzzedineAlaïa, @pieter_mulier obsesses with the idea of construction and rhe body merging fashion, sculpture and architecture engineering.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 12, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, raincoat, cloak, magazine and text that says '2027 - 1962 151 The Return of Cristóbal's Couture at Balenciaga'.

posts.caption:
#Balenciaga Spring 2027 may be #PierpaoloPiccioli’s clearest vision for the brand yet. Less about direct references, it focuses on #CristóbalBalenciaga’s philosophy of construction and the relationship between fabric and the body.
References to the house’s historic balloon, drape, and cocoon shapes suggest a welcome return to its heritage, but reengineered for modern life. Through new textile innovations, the collection emphasises lightness, with entire looks weighing less than a kilogram. @pppiccioli wants to demonstrate how #HauteCouture principles can be relevant to everyday life. Ordinary wardrobe staples are elevated through couture-level construction.
What do you think of the new Balenciaga so far?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 14, 2026. May be an image of dress, gown and text that says 'I'm Still Spiralling Over These Alaia Dresses'.

posts.caption:
One of my favourite signatures of #SarahBurton, and something I’m excited to see her continue at #Givenchy is her extraordinary couture fringe technique. First fully realised for #AlexanderMcQueen Fall 2018, she drew inspiration from insects, butterflies, and metamorphosis to create the  butterfly-wing gown, dissolving into cascading silk threads.
These garments are so fascinating because of the level of craftsmanship required. The threads are dyed, blended, layered, and hand-applied to create subtle gradients of colour and movement. I had the chance to see one IRL and the delicacy of the work is unbelievable. The fringe requires constant brushing and arrangement, with even the smallest movement causing the threads to tangle, making the garments feel almost alive.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 14, 2026. May be an image of dress, gown and text that says 'I'm Still Spiralling Over These Alaia Dresses'.

posts.caption:
One of my favourite, and most hauntingly beautiful details from #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli couture collections was the sheer crinoline veils/face masks, from Fall 2024. Wrapped around the face and head they functioned as sculptural forms. The material appeared almost weightless, creating distortions that blurred the contours of the face and transformed the models into embryonic works of art. Makeup by @patstingz, hair by @guidopalau
Also reminded me of #GarethPugh’s Spring 2013 collection, which featured sheer mesh and stretched fabrics wrapped around the head and face, or #MarcJacobs Spring 2019. Major inspo for brides looking for an unconventional wedding veil!
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 14, 2026. May be an image of dress, gown and text that says 'I'm Still Spiralling Over These Alaia Dresses'.

posts.caption:
If you have any of these bags, please step forward… #KarlLagerfeld was known for his larger-than-life shows, and the spectacle didn’t just stop at the sets, but translated into his absurd yet highly collectible bags.
Think of the iconic hula hoop bag from Spring 2013, which Karl joked was designed for the beach so you could stick it into the sand and hang your towel from it (which fyi someone is currently selling on eBay for £145K.) Then there was the supermarket show, complete with milk carton bags and the #Chanel chain basket, or the Spring 2019 flap bag filled with sand.
The bags became souvenirs from those fantasy worlds and now #MatthieuBlazy is continuing that tradition, turning handbags into collectible objects that blur the line between fashion and art.
Which one is your favourite?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 14, 2026. May be an image of dress, gown and text that says 'I'm Still Spiralling Over These Alaia Dresses'.

posts.caption:
This has to be one of my favourite editorial shoots I've seen this year, and that's probably because it was shot by #SzilveszterMako, whose images feel closer to theatre and painting than traditional fashion photography.
His editorials often have a dreamlike quality because of their painterly approach. Through dramatic lighting, strong contrasts between light and shadow he gives his subjects an almost sculptural presence. The clothing blends seamlessly into the composition, contributing to the overall mood and becoming an extension of the subject rather than something just being worn.
#Rihanna shot by @szilvesztermako for @ee72, styled by @edward_enninful wearing #Schiaparelli #Chanel #Dior Alexander McQueen & Bottega Veneta
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on June 14, 2026. May be an image of dress, gown and text that says 'I'm Still Spiralling Over These Alaia Dresses'.

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha
#Ironman #Philippines #sports full Ironman challenge, women in sports, women in endurance sports, mumbai sports, Chennai sports, IIT Madras student, women in STEM, ironman Kona, triathlon, marathon, menstrual awareness

In a recent example of women making a landmark move, IPS officer Ananya Awasthi has been appointed as the Aide-de-Camp (ADC) to the Governor of Odisha. Awasthi, a 2022-batch IPS officer of the Odisha cadre, previously served as Assistant Commissioner of Police (ACP), UPD, Cuttack.
A formal induction ceremony was held at Lok Bhavan, where Governor Dr Hari Babu Kambhampati welcomed Awasthi to her new role as the Police ADC.
Ananya Awasthi’s appointment indicates the growing presence of women in leadership. She has become an inspiration for many young girls, as she became the first woman officer to serve as ADC to the Governor of Odisha.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more
#ananyaawasthi #adc #odisha #ips

posts.caption:
The Embera indigenous community is one of the largest indigenous communities in Colombia. The young girls of the Embera community are subjected to female genital mutilation.
But on June 10, Colombia scripted history by passing a law aimed at preventing and eradicating female genital mutilation. With this decision, the country becomes the first in Latin America to criminalise this practice.
Female Genital Mutilation (FGM) is the procedure that involves cutting, removing, piercing, or injuring external female genital organs without medical justification.
The law has become a regional milestone that addresses gender-based violence, which has affected more than 230 million girls and women worldwide.
“We cannot let our guard down—the greater challenge is ensuring the law is implemented so that no girl is subjected to this practice in Colombia," said Leandra Becerra, Legal and Advocacy Advisor for Equality Now in Latin America and the Caribbean.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more
#colombia #fgm #emberá Colombia, Female genital mutilation, embera indigenous community, latin america, eradication of female genital mutilation

posts.caption:
Few romantic tropes have endured quite like the love triangle. From Elena navigating her feelings for two brothers in The Vampire Diaries to Belly caught between Conrad and Jeremiah in The Summer I Turned Pretty, we’re fascinated by stories where love isn’t straightforward. We pick sides, argue over who belongs with whom, and convince ourselves that the right choice is just around the corner.
The trope works because it reflects something deeply human: feelings are rarely as tidy as we’d like them to be. Contrary to what television often suggests, people don’t always fall out of love with one person before developing feelings for another.
Sometimes a relationship is already struggling when someone new enters the picture. Sometimes a friendship becomes something more. Sometimes a person is trying to move forward while still carrying pieces of their past.
That’s often how love triangles begin—not with bad intentions, but with overlap. Of course, what looks romantic on screen feels very different when you’re living it. In television dramas, love triangles are fuelled by chemistry, longing and dramatic declarations.
In reality, they’re more likely to involve uncertainty, guilt and endless second-guessing. One person is trying to figure out what they feel. Someone else is hoping to be chosen. Another is struggling to let go.
Everyone is waiting for clarity, and nobody seems to have it.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read full article by Shahzeen Shivdasani, Relationship Expert and Author of Love, Lust & Lemons. | Views expressed by the author are their own.
#bollywood #tvd #tsitp love story, romance, cocktail 2, kriti sanon, shahid kapoor, rashmika mandanna, movies, lov triangle movies, deepika padukone, saif ali khan, diana penty, the summer i turned pretty, the vampire diaries, jeramiah belly conrad bonrad jelly nina dobrev ian somerhalder , shah rukh khan, srk kajol rani mukerji

posts.caption:
Some weddings you photograph. And some you become a part of.
Aishwarya and Umapathy’s wedding was the second kind - where the warmth of the Sarja and Ramaiah families made everyone around them feel less like guests or vendors and more like family. The kind of celebration where the love in the room is so palpable it finds its way into every single frame without being asked.
That is the hardest thing to manufacture. And the most beautiful thing to witness.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this South Indian wedding truly unforgettable was how completely every element came together - the silk and gold of impeccably draped outfits, the florals holding their own against the grandeur of the setting, and two families opening their hearts wide enough to make everyone around them feel the joy as their own.
Some weddings are events.
And some are experiences - the kind that stay with everyone who was there, not just the couple at the centre of it all. 🌟

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
Celebrating the rise of the Godawan began with a simple question: how does a bird return to public memory?
A bird that builds its nest directly on the ground is especially vulnerable when people keep taking over its habitat. Once considered a candidate for India’s national bird, the Great Indian Bustard, locally known as Godawan, has gradually disappeared from public consciousness over the decades due to habitat loss and human encroachment.
Today, its conservation story is gaining renewed attention. In Rajasthan, breeding and hatchery programmes have brought cautious progress, with new hatchlings emerging in protected facilities. Conservation efforts combine habitat restoration, protected nesting areas, water management, predator control, captive breeding, and community-led monitoring of breeding grounds.
On World Environment Day, one of India’s most iconic monuments became a canvas for this conservation story, as Qutb Minar hosted projection mapping that highlighted the Godawan’s ecological role in India’s grassland ecosystems and the challenges facing its survival, with support from Godawan Estuary Premium Water.
posts.alt_text:
When Shivan Bhatiya (@shivanbhatiya) and Narresh Kukreja (@narresh) launched their label in 2010, Indian fashion was expanding into new lifestyle categories, while luxury swimwear and holiday dressing still sat at the edges of the designer conversation.

Their proposition was colour-blocked swimwear, resortwear that celebrated the silhouette, and the bikini sari, a garment attuned to both fantasy and the evolving Indian wardrobe.

Sixteen years later, Shivan & Narresh has stretched leisure into occasion wear, cruise wear, après-ski, home, accessories and objects, turning a category into a worldview.

For The Established’s latest episode of In The Studio, the designers opened the doors to their Gurugram headquarters. Across four floors, design, the leather atelier, embroidery, merchandising, marketing, production and finishing sit in close conversation. Ideas move quickly from sketch to swatch, moodboard to garment rack.

Shivan & Narresh began with the holiday wardrobe. The studio suggests the next chapter is the leisure world around it.

posts.caption:
Solo dining in India still comes with unsolicited concern. People assume you’ve been stood up, abandoned by friends, or are being brave in a way nobody asked for.
But younger Indians are changing the script. Gen Z and millennials are showing up alone at restaurants, runs, quizzes, food walks, gigs and climbing walls. At Bangalore Climbing Initiatives, nearly 80 per cent of people come solo. Restaurants are adding counter seating and smaller formats so a table for one feels less like a logistical problem.
The solo hang is no longer just about being alone. It is about choosing your time, your interests and the people you might meet along the way.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang ( @kkuenzang)
#solodining #solodates #urbanloneliness #genzindia #thirdspaces
posts.alt_text:
Celebrating the rise of the Godawan began with a simple question: how does a bird return to public memory?

A bird that builds its nest directly on the ground is especially vulnerable when people keep taking over its habitat. Once considered a candidate for India’s national bird, the Great Indian Bustard, locally known as Godawan, has gradually disappeared from public consciousness over the decades due to habitat loss and human encroachment.

Today, its conservation story is gaining renewed attention. In Rajasthan, breeding and hatchery programmes have brought cautious progress, with new hatchlings emerging in protected facilities. Conservation efforts combine habitat restoration, protected nesting areas, water management, predator control, captive breeding, and community-led monitoring of breeding grounds.

On World Environment Day, one of India’s most iconic monuments became a canvas for this conservation story, as Qutb Minar hosted projection mapping that highlighted the Godawan’s ecological role in India’s grassland ecosystems and the challenges facing its survival, with support from Godawan Estuary Premium Water.

posts.caption:
Depression is often described through what it takes away: energy, sleep, appetite, motivation and a sense of self. Far less often is it discussed through the lens of intimacy.
A reduced sex drive is one of the most common experiences associated with depression, yet it remains largely absent from conversations around mental health. For many people, the loss of desire arrives alongside emotional exhaustion, physical fatigue and the daily effort of simply getting through the day. The challenges can continue even after treatment begins, with antidepressants carrying sexual side effects that many patients are unaware of until they experience them.
The impact extends beyond the individual, shaping relationships, communication and self-perception in ways that are rarely acknowledged. As therapist Aanandita Vagahani puts it: “It’s often one of the first things to go and one of the last things to come back—and that has enormous consequences for relationships and self-worth.”
Read more about how depression and sex reshape intimacy by Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia) at the link in bio
#Depression #MentalHealth #SexualHealth #IntimacyMatters #TheEstablished

posts.caption:
Skincare has moved from bottles to jars and from serums to supplements. Peptide supplements are now headlining the edible skincare era, promising beauty from within.
But dermatologists caution: while oral collagen peptides show modest improvements in hydration and wrinkles, the claims around hair growth and miracle fixes are overstated.
The bigger story? Supplements are becoming lifestyle products, consumed by 70% of Indians without doctor consultation, driven by marketing that often blurs science.
The future of beauty may be edible, but it’s also a reminder to question the hype.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#Peptides #Supplements #SkinHealth #CollagenPeptides #BeautyCulture

posts.caption:
India entered the decade with a roadmap for managing its stray-dog population. The National Action Plan for Rabies Elimination, launched in 2021, aligned the country with the global goal of ending dog-mediated human rabies deaths by 2030. Revised Animal Birth Control Rules followed in 2023, reaffirming sterilisation and vaccination as the foundation of population management.
Then came 2025. Amid growing concern over dog attacks, the Supreme Court briefly backed the relocation of stray dogs in Delhi-NCR before returning to a framework centred on sterilisation and immunisation. Months later, it directed authorities to remove dogs from schools, hospitals, railway stations and bus stands. In 2026, the court permitted euthanasia for rabid, incurably ill and demonstrably dangerous dogs.
Yet every turn in the debate points back to the same question: what happens when court orders move faster than municipal capacity? Until vaccination, sterilisation and waste-management systems work at scale, each new attack risks restarting the cycle.

posts.caption:
Bring me here ASAP. Built between 1902 and 1930, Lluís Domènech i Montaner designed Hospital de la Santa Creu i Sant Pau around a radical idea for its time: that sunlight, gardens, color, and beautiful architecture could actively help heal the human body.
While a modern hospital still operates next door, the original Art Nouveau pavilions have since become a UNESCO World Heritage Site: a sprawling “garden city” of tiled facades, mosaic details, palm-lined pathways, and stained glass flooded with natural light.
A genuinely therapeutic place to spend an afternoon.
#Barcelona #ArtNouveau #Architecture #Travel #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Thirty minutes after this interview was supposed to begin, Vicky Kaushal’s (@vickykaushal09) tall, statuesque frame bursts in through the door at the Matrix office in Khar, Mumbai. “So sorry, bro,” he says, shaking this reporter’s hand with both of his. His day has been busy—his son’s visa was to be sorted, he couldn’t leave his wife alone, and there’s repair work going on in his building, he says, sounding like your average young husband and father with his daily household chores to take care of, not some Bollywood star with a platoon of staff to handle his every whim.
Since becoming a father, Kaushal says he’s come to understand and appreciate “a lot more” of the work his parents did in raising him. “It occurs to me at the most random moments that that [instilling the right values] is now my responsibility towards my son,” he says. “He is going to learn by observing how his parents are thinking, talking, and behaving. So, I’ve realised whatever you want to see reflected in him has to come in your actions rather than words.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
My pass time of choice during the rains? Online shopping 🤭
We’ve all been bested by the rain—soaked in an expected downpour, splashed on while waiting for our Ubers, drenched during a walk with the dog... But if you’re outfitted with the right gear, there’s no reason to fear the monsoon.
Luckily, many of this season’s coolest trends are perfectly suited for wet weather. Whether it’s the sudden obsession with retro windbreakers, trench coats, flip-flops, or the fact that the It shoes of the summer are both updates on the classic jelly sandal—Celine’s new PVC slipper, which comes with its Triomphe logo at the toe, and Jimmy Choo’s juicy, sparkly kitten heel—staying dry has never looked so stylish.
Head to the link in bio for out full list of items to add to cart ahead of the rains.
@celine @mango @prada @crocs @vegnonveg @maisonalaia @chanelofficial @loewe @gucci @capsulindia @bershka @diesel @stussy

posts.caption:
Before Hamilton had audiences rapping in the aisles and before The Phantom of the Opera became synonymous with theatrical spectacle, there was Cats (@catsthemusical). What began as a collection of poems about eccentric felines in TS Eliot’s Old Possum’s Book of Practical Cats evolved into one of the most successful musicals ever staged.
Since its 1981 West End debut, Andrew Lloyd Webber’s adaptation has captivated audiences with its blend of music, movement, and imagination, turning characters such as Grizabella and Macavity into musical-theatre icons. This year, 45 years after its premiere, the Jellicle Cats are finally making their India debut at Mumbai’s Nita Mukesh Ambani Cultural Centre (@nmacc.india).
At the link in bio, @sheyakurian with everything you need to know about the show and its India debut.

posts.caption:
you’d think there isn’t much left to do with the Louis Vuitton Monogram. 130 years after its creation, the House is proving otherwise.
meet Monogram Emblème: a new jacquard canvas inspired by the original trunk fabrics, reworked with a richer texture, denser pattern and an almost embroidered finish. subtle, but it’s the kind of update luxury houses are the best at.
the new signature arrives on classics including the Alma, Speedy, Neverfull, Noé and Keepall, alongside fresh shades pulled from Louis Vuitton’s historic colour archives.
not every reinvention needs to be loud. sometimes it’s about making an icon feel new again.
#TFO #louisvuitton #monogram #lv #fashion

posts.caption: The Atlas Of Affluence, the definitive white paper on the Rise of Indian Luxury and what it means across categories, culture, craft and new models of aspirations. Launching today.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Voice Of Fashion on March 18, 2026 tagging @kimidangor, @anannya_s_, @unnatisaini, @jaspreetchandok, and @shefatwork. May be an image of poster and magazine.

posts.caption:
A few weeks ago, when I walked into the new 11:11 / eleven eleven store in Delhi’s Lodhi Market, my usually taut focus zone began to elasticate rapidly. No one product or person angles for dominance here, despite the multiplicity of elements. A sturdy yet malleable bamboo arch peacefully burdened with ready-to-wear, indigo dyeing vats, ceramic miniatures, ruralism through architecture and fashion contemporaneity populate the space. In merchandise categories you have hand-painted sneakers, bandhani tunics, dresses and shirts—the brand’s signature pieces—appliquéd jackets, Kala Cotton denims, pyjamas and bed covers. Designer Shani Himanshu’s appliqué pieces look three-dimensional, but they are the godchildren of the multidimensional texture of his space. House colour Indigo runs through the store—as hue, intent and process.
One of my earlier articles on Himanshu’s work, It Takes a Village to Make a Dress, took off its boots to come and sit with me in the store. Clay, Kala Cotton and craftsmanship become the vocabulary of modernity. It is expressed through architecture, engineering and precision in the space, part of a 80-year-old structure.
The word “Blueprint” in the headline here is ironic because blue is an entire philosophy here. This is a maker’s studio masquerading as a fashion store, a republic where process outranks product. The products are not conclusions; they are outcomes. Painstaking, creative making is the main event.
The massy chronology of fashion, where product rules—has been rearranged. Artisanal skill, dyeing, bamboo and brick come before merchandising. The store assumes that knowledge—of the designer and artisan—and aesthetics possess intrinsic cultural value and must be exhibited.
From the retail area, you cross an indigo-vat passage to a bespoke fittings corner, where a glass ceiling pulls natural light in to play “fashion-fashion”. This is a deceptively simple space. Instead of modernising craft, Himanshu lets contemporaneity emerge from craft itself. Most stores sell objects. This one tells you how objects come into being. Some may simply come for the clothes and that would be perfectly legitimate.
🖋️ @shefatwork

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on June 13, 2026. May be an image of poster and text that says 'The Goddess Project The First Makers Some goddesses wove. Some goddesses were woven for. Edition III: South & East Asia veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
Every culture created a goddess who presided over making, but not all of them did it the same way.
This edition introduced two categories.
The first: goddesses whose mythology is explicitly about weaving (documented, primary sources, this is what the texts actually say).
The second: goddesses that weavers chose: figures of creativity, wisdom and skill that women at the loom reached toward deliberately, even though weaving wasn’t the centre of their story.
Both categories are true, just in different ways.
And here is what I keep coming back to across all three editions of the First Makers series. You never find a GOD of weaving. Not once, across any culture. Every time, the act of making cloth belongs to the feminine divine.
That is the oldest memory we have of what women’s knowledge meant.
Full episode on The Culture of Cloth podcast 🎧 link in bio. Start there if you’re new here.
Save this and share it with someone who makes things.
Cover art: Court Ladies Preparing Newly Woven Silk, a Chinese silk painting by Emperor Huizong of Song, early 12th century.

posts.caption:
Six months of research, four in-depth online sessions and one of the oldest stories ever told 📕
I’m joining @readingsbooks Big Book Club for a deep dive into Homer’s Odyssey. It’s an online session running every Thursday from 18 June through to the Christopher Nolan film screening on 16 July.
Penelope. Athena. Circe. Helen. Nausicaa. These women have been living in my research for months and this series is the moment I get to go deeper with scholars who’ve spent their careers inside this text.
I’ll be sharing Substack reflections after each session and the first one is free, with sessions 2–5 are for paid subscribers.
Link in bio for the Readings online membership.
In partnership with @readingsbooks

posts.caption: Rosa Genoni in 1908 design her manifesto dress named TANAGRA as the little women terracotta statues discovered in TANAGRA Beozia few year earlier . It is a dress with our corset , it is possible to wear it in different ways according to what a women needs and time of the day . It shaped the women figure in a innovative style , it was a revolution with roots in classical Greek history . Genoni herself went in Rome to the first Italian Women Meeting wearing it . She spoke about freedom of women , and women rights and women working rights .

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.

The 17 SDGs in detail:
1-Eradicating Poverty: To end extreme poverty in all its forms.
2-Zero Hunger and Sustainable Agriculture: To end hunger, achieve food security and improve nutrition by promoting sustainable agriculture.
3-Health and Wellness: To guarantee a healthy life and promote well-being for everyone at all ages.
4-Quality Education: To guarantee inclusive, equitable, and quality education, and to promote lifelong learning opportunities.
5-Gender Equality: Achieving gender equality and empowering all women and girls.
6-Drinking Water and Sanitation: To guarantee the availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
7-Renewable and Affordable Energy: To ensure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy sources for all.
8-Decent Work and Economic Growth: To promote sustained, inclusive and sustainable economic growth, full and productive employment, and decent work for all.
9-Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure: To build resilient infrastructure, promote inclusive and sustainable industrialization, and foster innovation.
10-Reducing Inequalities: To reduce inequalities within and between countries.
11-Sustainable Cities and Communities: To make cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient and sustainable.
12-Responsible Consumption and Production: Adopt sustainable production and consumption patterns.
13-Climate Action: Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts.
14-arine Life: To conserve and sustainably use the oceans, seas and marine resources.
15-Life on Earth: To protect, restore and promote the sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems, manage forests sustainably, combat desertification, halt and reverse land degradation and halt biodiversity loss.
16-Peace, Justice and Effective Institutions: To promote peac

posts.caption:
Bottega Veneta is betting on mycelium. Earlier this month, the Kering-owned brand released a limited collection of small leather goods swapping the bovine leather in its signature intrecciato weave for Ephea, a mycelium-based alternative by Italian biomaterials startup Sqim. The pieces are part of the fall 2026 men’s collection by creative director Louise Trotter.
Bottega Veneta’s mycelium collection is a step in the right direction, says Kering chief sustainability and institutional affairs officer Marie-Claire Daveu. Get the full story from @‌BellaWebb via the link in bio.

Kinal Patel and Sanjeeve Sivakumar had pictured a wedding in Rajasthan, but decided on Marrakech. The mehndi at Palais Soleiman opened the weekend with Moroccan folk dancers and a traditional Moroccan buffet. As a nod to Sivakumar’s heritage, the couple found a South Indian chef in Marrakech who made mutton rolls, a Tamil delicacy, to serve as canapés. Patel’s first bridal look was a custom Kanjivaram silk sari that she had designed herself, with the patterns and details woven from scratch by her father-in-law’s sari business in Chennai. Later, for the Gujarati ceremony, she changed into a heavily detailed Marwar Couture lehenga weighing 16kg. Head to the link in bio to see their wedding up-close.

posts.caption:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.
She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”
Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.
For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile.
Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. posts.caption: Major change is afoot in Sweden, a fact highlighted by the two shows that bookended Stockholm Fashion Week, by provocative and kinky Gullbo on one side and the ruminatively minimalist Leoní on the other. Not only did these collections represent two vastly different ends of an aesthetic spectrum, but they also marked a generational shift (which was also evident in street style). At the link in bio, find a lowdown of the interesting, youthful, melange that was STHLMFW 2026.

posts.caption: Armani for a certain type of tapped-in shopper, Desert Vintage on the Lower East Side is akin to nirvana. Zachary Witter is the store’s longest-serving employee, save for its current owners, Roberto Cowan and Salima Boufelfel, and is in charge of categorizing and dating the rare and glorious treasures that come through the shop’s doors. For those discerning shoppers entranced not by fresh-off-the-runway finds (too common, too mass-produced), but, instead, garments with history and legacy embedded in their seams, these are holy grails. “When I’m working with customers that are looking at really high-end pieces here, they’re looking for me to sell them a story, not a garment,” Witter tells Vogue. Meet the archivist IDing all the designer goods at New York’s hottest vintage shop at the link in bio.

posts.caption: One of the silver linings of being a frequent traveler—beyond the miles accrued—is that packing isn’t so much a chore as it is muscle memory. “I have it down to a science,” says Ilona Hamer, co-founder of Australian resortwear label Matteau of her dialed system. Last year alone, the stylist and consultant spent about 250 days on the go. At the link in bio, for the latest installment of #MyPackingStyle, Hamer shares what she packs for a holiday in the sun.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Shopping on June 14, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people playing soccer and text that says '5 outfits to sport during the World Cup'.

posts.caption: If summer’s sandals trends share a common thread, it’s a refusal to choose between any one aesthetic. Designers have traded the exclusively minimalist mood of recent summers for options that encourage self-expression. Whether it’s Le Monde Béryl’s pale blue beaded sandals, Gucci’s sporty heeled thongs, or Ancient Greek Sandals’ jelly flops, the chicest sandal trends of 2026 are personal rather than prescriptive. Discover them all at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Shopping on June 13, 2026. May be an image of anklet, sandals, slingback shoes and text.

posts.caption: “The best evenings always have a little mix of the familiar and the unexpected,” says @arussotti, founder and creative director of Brooklyn-based decor brand @Porta_nyc. “I’ve never once remembered a dinner because everything was perfect. I remember it because it felt lovely and nobody wanted to leave.” Recently, on one of the first truly warm June evenings, she put this entertaining philosophy into practice with an intimate backyard party in Brooklyn’s Boerum Hill. She enlisted the help of Arroces Street Paella to whip up two varieties of the dish, so she could spend the evening mingling with guests. Discover all of her tips and tricks for hosting this summer at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Shopping on June 14, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people playing soccer and text that says '5 outfits to sport during the World Cup'.

Reminding us once again that sometimes simplicity is the answer, shop her recent look at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
: 💎 Which Bridal Necklace Suits Your Face Shape Best? 👰✨
The secret to a stunning bridal look isn't just the jewellery—it's choosing pieces that complement your unique features. From elegant chokers for long faces to regal rani haars for round faces, the right necklace can beautifully enhance your natural beauty.
✨ Oval Face? You're lucky—almost every style works!
✨ Round Face? Go for longer silhouettes.
✨ Square Face? Softer curves create balance.
✨ Heart-Shaped Face? Medium-length pieces are your best friend.
✨ Diamond Face? Statement chokers were made for you.
✨ Long Face? Broad necklaces add the perfect proportion.
Save this guide before your jewellery shopping begins and find the necklace that was truly made for you. 💍
👇 Tell us your face shape in the comments, and we'll suggest your perfect bridal necklace!

posts.caption:
@camimendes is having one of the biggest years in her career so far. A major summer blockbuster, the second feature film from her production company, and wedding planning are all on the roster for 2026. This month, Mendes steps into the sprawling world of Masters of the Universe as the sword-slinging Teela alongside Nicholas Galitzine, Idris Elba, and Jared Leto.
The project is a stretch from her days as Veronica Lodge in #Riverdale, which catapulted her into mainstream recognition nearly a decade ago. Mendes makes movies for “the girls and the gays,” she states lovingly, but her star power is only going to get bigger and brighter with Masters. “I’ve never been a part of something of this scale,” she says of the project.
In our Now Playing Issue, presented by @bvlgari, we chat with the actress about reinventing herself in this era of her life. Read it all in the full interview at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
There would be no Pope Heyward without @jonathandavissofficial. Much like his smart, careful, deeply moral Outer Banks character, Daviss knows what it feels like to carry the pressure of proving your worth and refusing to let yourself or anyone else down. “What really stood out to me about Pope in season 1 was this idea of expectations and pressure that gets put on a lot of young Black teens and teens in general,” he says. “There’s this pressure to succeed, and it was something I truly have been through.”
Daviss still thinks back to his early audition days before landing the life-changing role on OBX, when the constant need to prove himself as a young actor felt emotionally exhausting. His mindset then? “You only lose when you quit.”
Head to the link in our bio for the full download in our Now Playing Issue presented by @bvlgari, from what to expect from the fifth and final season of OBX to his upcoming portrayal of a young Snoop Dogg.

posts.caption:
@victoriamonet is trading her claws for butterfly wings. The three-time Grammy winner is the definition of a multi-hyphenate: a culinary school graduate, a mother, and, of course, a singer-songwriter with a nearly two-decade-spanning catalog to her name. Now, using her 37th birthday as a launching pad, Monét has cleared her mental space and scrubbed her socials to prepare for her next chapter—an album she’s dubbed her very own Thriller, a sonically surprising, genre-defying project that marks a softer evolution. “The jaguar exists within me always, but I’m feeling like a softer version of myself now,” she says.
After all, Monét’s critically acclaimed debut studio album Jaguar II was the ultimate learning lesson. “Once you have an accolade like that, people often look for a reason to discredit why you got it,” she explains. “I think—pending a Grammy happens again—I would do the opposite and bunker down in the studio.”
Step inside Monét’s world in our Now Playing Issue, presented by @bvlgari, at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Growing up with an identical twin sister can be both a blessing and a curse, according to our Editor in Residence @kristinamichelleang. “When we were young, our parents got a kick out of dressing us up in matching outfits. And for a long time, it stuck. Even when we were old enough to style ourselves, we dressed the same.” After college and moving apart, Kristina and her twin sister, @alyxandriaang’s styles started to differentiate. At 26, the two are fully embracing that looking like one other was and isn’t a negative thing—it was something that also made them unique. From statement glasses to lip stains, explore and shop the trends that both sisters love at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
“I did not want to lose myself,” @faouzia says.⁠
⁠
Last year, the 25-year-old singer-songwriter found herself at a crossroads. She had been dropped from her label, relationships in her personal life were unraveling, and she was being asked to leave music behind.⁠
⁠
“I was being told to be something that I was not,” she recalls. “I was asked to abandon what made me, me…I cannot move on in my life without music, and I cannot move on in my life without being an artist. That’s not something that really leaves you.”⁠
⁠
Instead, she wrote.⁠
⁠
The result was “Film Noir,” an album shaped by a period when, as she puts it, “my whole life fell apart.” ⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @ryma___________⁠
Photo: @dandoperalski/WWD

posts.caption:
Paris Fashion Week is going to need another closing act.⁠
⁠
KidSuper, who was supposed to close men’s fashion week with a runway show on June 28, has opted to remain in the States and show his spring collection in Miami during the FIFA World Cup.⁠
⁠
This marks the first time the buzzy Brooklyn-based brand will show its main collection outside of Paris since being accepted to the official calendar in June of 2022. Before that, he had shown off-calendar for four years.⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @jeanpalmieri

posts.caption:
Designers and executives fondly remembered Etta Froio, former senior vice president, executive editor and associate publisher of Fairchild Publications, parent of WWD, for her fashion knowledge, clever wit and grace under pressure. Froio was also praised by colleagues for her kindness, patience, ability to teach her staff and successfully manage a frenetic newsroom with an extremely calm demeanor. Froio died early Thursday in Shewsbury, N.J. at age 94.⁠
⁠
Read the tributes at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @lisajlockwood⁠
Photo: Tony Palmieri/Fairchild Archive
